text: A 64-year-old man has progressive shortness of breath, orthopnea, bilateral lung crackles, and pitting leg edema. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: CHF: dyspnea + orthopnea + bilateral crackles + peripheral edema + elevated BNP. Fluid overload from impaired cardiac pumping. Tx: furosemide + ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker.

text: Which medication reduces mortality in systolic heart failure?
rationale: ACE inhibitors reduce mortality by decreasing afterload and preventing cardiac remodeling. Diuretics relieve symptoms only. Beta-blockers and spironolactone also reduce mortality.

text: A patient has sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back and unequal BP in both arms. Diagnosis?
rationale: Aortic dissection: sudden tearing pain + radiation to back + BP difference between arms + widened mediastinum on CXR. Do NOT anticoagulate. Dx: CT angiography immediately.

text: Best initial imaging for suspected aortic dissection in a hemodynamically stable patient?
rationale: CT angiography is fast, widely available, and highly sensitive (98%) for aortic dissection. Transesophageal echo is alternative if CT unavailable. CXR may show widened mediastinum but is not diagnostic.

text: A 70-year-old smoker presents with hemoptysis, 10 kg weight loss, and hoarseness. CXR shows a hilar mass. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Lung cancer red flags: smoker + hemoptysis + weight loss + hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement) + hilar mass. Next step: CT chest then bronchoscopy/biopsy.

text: Which type of lung cancer is currently the most common overall (including non-smokers)?
rationale: Adenocarcinoma: #1 lung cancer overall. Peripheral location. Found in smokers and non-smokers. EGFR mutations targetable. Squamous cell: central, cavitating. Small cell: most aggressive, neuroendocrine.

text: Best lung cancer screening test for high-risk patients (age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, currently smoking or quit <15 years)?
rationale: Annual low-dose CT (LDCT) reduces lung cancer mortality by ~20% in high-risk patients. CXR is NOT adequate for screening. Canadians: start screening at age 50.

text: A 22-year-old man has periumbilical pain that migrated to RLQ over 12 hours, fever 38.4°C, and rebound at McBurney point. WBC 14,000. Diagnosis?
rationale: Classic appendicitis: periumbilical → RLQ migration + rebound at McBurney point + fever + leukocytosis. Alvarado score guides Tx. CT confirms if unclear. Tx: laparoscopic appendectomy.

text: Which physical sign is most classic for acute appendicitis?
rationale: McBurney point: 1/3 from ASIS to umbilicus. Rovsing sign: LLQ pressure → RLQ pain. Murphy sign = cholecystitis. Cullen (periumbilical bruising) + Grey Turner (flank bruising) = hemorrhagic pancreatitis.

text: A 55-year-old woman has RUQ pain, fever 39°C, and jaundice (Charcot triad). BP is 80/50 and she is confused. Diagnosis?
rationale: Reynolds pentad = Charcot triad + hypotension + AMS = severe ascending cholangitis. Life-threatening emergency. Tx: IV antibiotics immediately + urgent ERCP for biliary decompression.

text: A 68-year-old man has sudden onset right arm and leg weakness and slurred speech. What percentage of strokes are ischemic?
rationale: 85% of strokes are ischemic (thrombotic or embolic). 15% hemorrhagic. CT head distinguishes: ischemic = normal early; hemorrhagic = hyperdense (white) blood immediately.

text: What is the single most important modifiable risk factor for stroke?
rationale: Hypertension = #1 modifiable risk factor for ALL stroke types. Treating HTN reduces stroke risk by 40%. AF is a major cause of cardioembolic stroke but HTN overall is #1 modifiable factor.

text: A 45-year-old presents with polyuria, polydipsia, blurry vision. Random blood glucose is 14.2 mmol/L. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: DM: polyuria + polydipsia + hyperglycemia. Random glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L with symptoms = diagnostic. Also: fasting ≥7.0, HbA1c ≥6.5%, or 2h OGTT ≥11.1.

text: Which test is used to BOTH diagnose diabetes AND monitor long-term glucose control?
rationale: HbA1c ≥6.5% = diabetes diagnosis. Also monitors 3-month average glucose control. Target in treated T2DM: <7%. Reflects glycosylated hemoglobin (proportional to average blood glucose).

text: A 52-year-old is newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. HbA1c is 8.2%. No cardiovascular disease. What is the first-line pharmacotherapy?
rationale: Metformin: first-line T2DM. Reduces hepatic glucose production. No hypoglycemia, modest weight loss, cardiovascular neutral, cheap. If HbA1c >10% or symptomatic: start insulin. With CVD/CKD: add SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA.

text: A 19-year-old has severe sore throat, fever 39°C, and white tonsillar exudate. No cough. Rapid strep test is positive. Most likely organism?
rationale: Group A Strep: Centor criteria = exudate + fever + tender anterior cervical nodes + NO cough. EBV: exudate + posterior cervical lymphadenopathy + splenomegaly. Tx: Penicillin V to prevent rheumatic fever.

text: What is the first-line antibiotic for Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis?
rationale: Penicillin V x10 days = gold standard for GAS pharyngitis (prevents acute rheumatic fever and glomerulonephritis). Amoxicillin acceptable. Azithromycin if penicillin-allergic. Do NOT use fluoroquinolones.

text: A 38-year-old immigrant has 4-week cough, fever, night sweats, and 10 kg weight loss. CXR shows upper lobe cavitary lesion. Diagnosis?
rationale: TB: cough >3 weeks + constitutional symptoms + upper lobe cavitary lesion. Dx: sputum AFB smear x3 + culture + CXR. Tx: RIPE (Rifampin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol) x2 months, then RI x4 months.

text: A 3-year-old child has barking cough, hoarse voice, and stridor worse at night. Low-grade fever. Most appropriate first-line treatment?
rationale: Croup: oral dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg) is first-line for ALL severity. Reduces duration and prevents return visits. Add racemic epinephrine nebulization if moderate-severe stridor at rest.

text: Most common cause of bronchiolitis requiring hospitalization in infants under 2 years?
rationale: RSV = #1 cause of bronchiolitis. Seasonal (winter). Treatment: supportive (hydration, O₂, suction). Palivizumab (RSV monoclonal antibody) for prophylaxis in premature infants.

text: Which neurotransmitter deficiency is primarily implicated in major depressive disorder?
rationale: Serotonin deficiency = primary monoamine hypothesis of depression. SSRIs increase synaptic serotonin. Also: norepinephrine and dopamine involved. Dopamine deficiency = Parkinson + ADHD + schizophrenia (negative symptoms).

text: A 26-year-old has recurrent unexpected panic attacks with palpitations, sweating, chest tightness. Cardiac workup is negative. First-line LONG-TERM treatment?
rationale: Panic disorder long-term: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) + CBT (gold standard combination). Benzodiazepines = SHORT-TERM acute attacks only (dependence risk). Beta-blockers = performance anxiety (situational).

text: A patient presents to ER with severe acute panic attack with trembling, palpitations, and fear of dying. Which drug provides fastest relief?
rationale: Benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam) = fastest relief of acute panic/anxiety (minutes). For chronic prevention: switch to SSRI. Avoid long-term benzodiazepines due to tolerance and dependence.

text: A 56-year-old man has smoked 1 pack/day for 35 years. He is currently smoking. What cancer screening is recommended?
options: No screening needed at this age
rationale: Annual low-dose CT: recommended for age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, currently smoking or quit <15 years. Reduces lung cancer mortality ~20%. Canadian guidelines 2023. CXR is NOT adequate screening.

text: A 60-year-old Jehovah's Witness who is fully competent refuses a blood transfusion needed for elective surgery. He understands he may bleed out. Most appropriate action?
options: Transfuse against his will to save his life
Get family consent to override him
Respect his refusal, document thoroughly, explore alternatives
Proceed with surgery without transfusion on standby
rationale: Competent adult autonomy: absolute right to refuse treatment, even life-saving. Assess and document capacity. Explore alternatives (cell saver, erythropoietin, accept lower Hgb). Family cannot override capable adult. Apology Act protects disclosure.

text: A 32-year-old woman presents with pleuritic chest pain that improves when she leans forward. ECG shows diffuse ST elevation. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute pericarditis causes sharp pleuritic chest pain relieved by sitting forward. ECG shows diffuse ST elevation and PR depression. Treatment is NSAIDs plus colchicine.

text: A 75-year-old man has syncope, angina, and dyspnea on exertion. Exam reveals a harsh systolic murmur radiating to the carotids. Diagnosis?
rationale: Aortic stenosis presents with the classic triad of syncope, angina, and dyspnea. The murmur radiates to the carotids. Severe symptomatic disease requires valve replacement.

text: A 24-year-old tall athlete collapses during exercise. Family history reveals sudden cardiac death. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy causes exertional syncope and sudden death in young athletes. It is often inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. A harsh systolic murmur increases with Valsalva.

text: A 68-year-old smoker has chronic dyspnea and barrel chest. Exam shows decreased breath sounds. Which COPD type is most likely?
rationale: Emphysema causes dyspnea, hyperinflation, decreased breath sounds, and barrel chest. It is associated with alveolar wall destruction. Smoking is the main risk factor.

text: A patient with community-acquired pneumonia has fever, productive cough, and pleuritic chest pain. Most common organism in adults?
rationale: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia in adults. It classically causes fever, productive cough, and lobar consolidation.

text: A 72-year-old hospitalized patient suddenly develops dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain after surgery. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Pulmonary embolism should be suspected after surgery or immobilization with sudden dyspnea and pleuritic pain. Risk factors include malignancy and recent travel. CT pulmonary angiography is the preferred test in stable patients.

text: A 40-year-old woman has chronic diarrhea, weight loss, and iron deficiency anemia. Anti-tTG antibodies are positive. Diagnosis?
rationale: Celiac disease causes malabsorption, chronic diarrhea, weight loss, and iron deficiency anemia. Tissue transglutaminase antibodies are commonly positive. Treatment is lifelong gluten-free diet.

text: A 28-year-old man has recurrent abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, and perianal fistulas. Diagnosis?
rationale: Crohn disease can affect any part of the GI tract and commonly causes transmural inflammation, fistulas, and skip lesions. Perianal disease strongly suggests Crohn disease.

text: A patient has bloody diarrhea and continuous colonic inflammation beginning in the rectum. Diagnosis?
rationale: Ulcerative colitis causes continuous mucosal inflammation beginning in the rectum and presents with bloody diarrhea. Toxic megacolon is a major complication.

text: A 68-year-old man develops sudden facial droop and inability to close his eye on one side. Forehead is involved. Diagnosis?
rationale: Bell palsy is a lower motor neuron facial nerve palsy affecting the entire side of the face including the forehead. Treatment includes corticosteroids and eye protection.

text: A 60-year-old woman has brief recurrent electric shock-like facial pain triggered by chewing. Diagnosis?
rationale: Trigeminal neuralgia causes sudden severe shock-like unilateral facial pain triggered by touching the face or chewing. Carbamazepine is first-line therapy.

text: A 22-year-old man has recurrent generalized tonic-clonic seizures. First-line long-term treatment for generalized seizures?
rationale: Valproate is a common first-line medication for generalized tonic-clonic seizures, though pregnancy status must be considered. Benzodiazepines are used acutely, not as sole long-term therapy.

text: A 34-year-old woman has polyuria, polydipsia, hypernatremia, and low urine osmolality. Symptoms improve with desmopressin. Diagnosis?
rationale: Central diabetes insipidus results from low ADH and responds to desmopressin. Patients have dilute urine and hypernatremia from free water loss.

text: A 52-year-old man has recurrent kidney stones, abdominal pain, and psychiatric symptoms. Calcium is elevated. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Primary hyperparathyroidism causes hypercalcemia with stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric overtones. Elevated PTH and calcium support the diagnosis.

text: A patient has confusion, constipation, and ECG changes. Serum calcium is 3.4 mmol/L. Initial management includes?
rationale: Severe hypercalcemia is initially treated with aggressive IV normal saline to restore volume and enhance calcium excretion. Additional therapy may include calcitonin or bisphosphonates.

text: A young adult has fever, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, sore throat, and splenomegaly. Monospot is positive. Diagnosis?
rationale: Infectious mononucleosis due to EBV presents with fever, sore throat, posterior cervical nodes, and splenomegaly. Patients should avoid contact sports because of splenic rupture risk.

text: A sexually active 22-year-old woman has lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, and fever. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pelvic inflammatory disease causes pelvic pain, fever, and cervical motion tenderness. Common organisms include Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Treat promptly to reduce infertility risk.

text: A 24-year-old man has urethral discharge. NAAT is positive for gonorrhea. Recommended treatment?
rationale: Gonorrhea is treated with ceftriaxone IM. Co-treatment for chlamydia is added if not excluded. Sexual partners also need treatment.

text: A 2-year-old has fever, conjunctivitis, cough, coryza, and Koplik spots, followed by a descending rash. Diagnosis?
rationale: Measles presents with cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, Koplik spots, and a descending rash. It is highly contagious. Vaccination is preventive.

text: A child has palpable purpura on the legs, abdominal pain, arthralgia, and hematuria after a URI. Diagnosis?
rationale: Henoch-Schönlein purpura, now called IgA vasculitis, presents with palpable purpura, abdominal pain, arthralgia, and renal involvement. It often follows an upper respiratory infection.

text: A 6-year-old has fever, sore throat, strawberry tongue, and a sandpaper rash. Diagnosis?
rationale: Scarlet fever is caused by Group A Streptococcus producing erythrogenic toxin. The rash feels like sandpaper and the tongue may appear strawberry-like.

text: A child with unrepaired ventricular septal defect develops cyanosis years later. This late complication is called?
rationale: Long-standing left-to-right shunts can cause pulmonary hypertension and reversal to a right-to-left shunt, producing Eisenmenger syndrome with cyanosis.

text: A newborn is cyanotic. CXR shows an egg-on-a-string appearance. Diagnosis?
rationale: Transposition of the great arteries classically shows an egg-on-a-string appearance on chest radiography. Prostaglandin E1 helps maintain ductal patency before surgery.

text: A 23-year-old woman has lower abdominal pain. Ultrasound shows an enlarged ovary with reduced blood flow. Diagnosis?
rationale: Ovarian torsion presents with sudden pelvic pain and decreased ovarian blood flow on Doppler ultrasound. It is a surgical emergency because ovarian necrosis can occur.

text: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with vaginal bleeding. Most important diagnosis to exclude?
rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise. Transvaginal ultrasound and endometrial biopsy are key investigations.

text: A pregnant woman at 28 weeks has BP 170/110, headache, and proteinuria. First-line seizure prophylaxis?
rationale: Severe preeclampsia requires magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis. Antihypertensives help control blood pressure, but magnesium prevents eclampsia.

text: A 32-year-old at 30 weeks gestation has fundal height larger than dates and polyhydramnios. She has not yet been screened. Most likely cause?
rationale: Gestational diabetes can cause fetal macrosomia and polyhydramnios. Screening is usually performed at 24 to 28 weeks with glucose testing.

text: A patient has a painful irreducible groin bulge with vomiting and abdominal distension. Diagnosis?
rationale: A painful irreducible hernia with bowel obstruction symptoms suggests incarceration or strangulation. This is a surgical emergency because bowel ischemia may develop.

text: A 58-year-old woman has fever, jaundice, and RUQ pain after gallstones. Next definitive procedure if cholangitis is suspected?
rationale: Ascending cholangitis requires biliary decompression, usually by ERCP. Antibiotics alone are not enough in severe or obstructive cases.

text: A hypotensive trauma patient has distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds, and low blood pressure. Diagnosis?
rationale: Beck triad of hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and distended neck veins suggests cardiac tamponade. Immediate pericardiocentesis may be lifesaving.

text: A trauma patient has absent breath sounds on one side, hypotension, tracheal deviation, and respiratory distress. Immediate management?
rationale: Tension pneumothorax is treated immediately with needle decompression followed by chest tube. Do not wait for imaging when the diagnosis is clinically obvious.

text: A 23-year-old medical student has persistent excessive worry about many daily events for more than 6 months, with insomnia and muscle tension. Diagnosis?
rationale: Generalized anxiety disorder features excessive uncontrollable worry for at least 6 months plus symptoms such as restlessness, insomnia, and muscle tension. SSRIs and CBT are effective.

text: A woman develops nightmares, flashbacks, and hypervigilance 2 months after a serious car accident. Diagnosis?
rationale: PTSD requires trauma exposure plus symptoms such as flashbacks, avoidance, and hyperarousal lasting more than 1 month. Trauma-focused psychotherapy is central treatment.

text: A man has intrusive contamination thoughts and washes his hands for hours daily despite knowing it is unreasonable. Diagnosis?
rationale: Obsessive-compulsive disorder features intrusive obsessions and repetitive compulsions performed to reduce anxiety. First-line treatment is SSRIs and exposure-response prevention therapy.

text: A 29-year-old woman develops low mood, tearfulness, and poor sleep beginning 2 weeks after delivery. No psychosis. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Postpartum depression usually occurs within weeks to months after delivery and causes persistent depressive symptoms. It is more severe and longer-lasting than postpartum blues.

text: A child of African ancestry has recurrent painful crises and dactylitis. Blood smear shows sickled cells. Diagnosis?
rationale: Sickle cell disease causes vaso-occlusive pain crises, dactylitis, and hemolytic anemia. Hydroxyurea reduces crises by increasing fetal hemoglobin.

text: A boy has recurrent hemarthroses and prolonged bleeding after minor trauma. PT is normal, PTT is prolonged. Diagnosis?
rationale: Hemophilia A is factor VIII deficiency and presents with hemarthroses and prolonged PTT. Treatment includes factor VIII replacement.

text: A 32-year-old woman has mucosal bleeding and heavy menstrual bleeding. Platelet count is low, PT/PTT are normal. Diagnosis?
rationale: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura causes isolated thrombocytopenia with mucocutaneous bleeding. PT and PTT are usually normal. Treatment may include steroids or IVIG.

text: A teenager has comedones, inflammatory papules, and pustules on the face. Diagnosis?
rationale: Acne vulgaris is characterized by comedones, papules, and pustules, especially in adolescents. Initial treatment often includes topical benzoyl peroxide or retinoids.

text: A 35-year-old woman has an erythematous facial rash with papules and pustules but no comedones. Symptoms worsen with hot drinks. Diagnosis?
rationale: Rosacea causes central facial erythema with papules and pustules without comedones. Triggers include alcohol, heat, and hot drinks. Metronidazole is a common treatment.

text: A patient has silvery scaly plaques on the extensor surfaces of elbows and knees. Diagnosis?
rationale: Psoriasis causes well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale, often on extensor surfaces. Nail pitting may also be present.

text: A patient with chronic kidney disease develops fatigue and normocytic anemia. Main cause?
rationale: CKD commonly causes normocytic anemia because the kidneys produce less erythropoietin. Treatment may include erythropoiesis-stimulating agents after iron status is checked.

text: A 70-year-old man with BPH develops acute urinary retention and rising creatinine. Initial management?
rationale: Postrenal obstruction from BPH causing urinary retention is initially managed with bladder catheterization. Relieving obstruction can improve renal function.

text: A 70-year-old woman has proximal muscle pain and morning stiffness in the shoulders and hips. ESR is elevated. Diagnosis?
rationale: Polymyalgia rheumatica causes proximal stiffness and pain in older adults with high ESR. It responds dramatically to low-dose corticosteroids. It is associated with giant cell arteritis.

text: A 50-year-old woman has dry eyes, dry mouth, and positive anti-Ro antibodies. Diagnosis?
rationale: Sjögren syndrome causes xerostomia and keratoconjunctivitis sicca. Anti-Ro and anti-La antibodies are often present. There is increased lymphoma risk.

text: Which screening test is recommended for average-risk colorectal cancer screening starting at age 50?
rationale: Colonoscopy is a standard colorectal cancer screening test for average-risk adults beginning around age 50 in many exam frameworks. It can both detect and remove precancerous polyps.

text: A 30-year-old healthcare worker sustains a needlestick injury from an unknown source. Immediate first step?
options: Wash area and report exposure immediately
rationale: After needlestick exposure, the site should be washed immediately and the exposure reported promptly so risk assessment and post-exposure prophylaxis can be arranged if needed.

text: A patient tells his doctor he intends to kill a specific person tomorrow. What is the physician's duty?
options: Warn the potential victim and notify authorities
Do nothing if notes are documented
rationale: When a patient poses an imminent threat to an identifiable person, confidentiality may be breached to warn the person and involve authorities. This is the duty to warn.

text: A confused elderly patient refuses urgent surgery. What must be assessed first?
rationale: Before accepting or overriding refusal, the physician must assess decision-making capacity. Capacity is task-specific and can fluctuate. If lacking, a substitute decision-maker is used.

text: An unconscious patient has no advance directive. The family disagrees about care. Best next step?
options: Discuss goals of care and identify legal substitute decision-maker
rationale: When no advance directive exists, clinicians should identify the legally authorized substitute decision-maker and discuss goals of care with the family based on the patient's values and best interests.

text: A 26-year-old woman has recurrent palpitations. ECG shows a short PR interval and delta wave. Diagnosis?
rationale: WPW syndrome shows a short PR interval and delta wave due to an accessory pathway. It predisposes to re-entrant tachyarrhythmias. Symptomatic cases are often treated with ablation.

text: A 68-year-old woman has fatigue and dyspnea. Exam reveals a holosystolic murmur radiating to the axilla. Diagnosis?
rationale: Mitral regurgitation classically causes a holosystolic murmur best heard at the apex and radiating to the axilla. Causes include mitral valve prolapse and ischemic papillary muscle dysfunction.

text: A 40-year-old man with HIV has progressive dyspnea, dry cough, and bilateral diffuse interstitial infiltrates. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia is common in immunocompromised patients, especially with HIV and low CD4 count. It often presents with dry cough, progressive dyspnea, and diffuse interstitial infiltrates.

text: A patient with COPD suddenly becomes very drowsy after receiving too much oxygen. Most likely explanation?
rationale: Some COPD patients rely partly on hypoxic drive, and excessive oxygen can worsen CO2 retention. Controlled oxygen therapy is recommended in severe COPD exacerbations.

text: A 45-year-old man has hematemesis after years of heavy alcohol use. Endoscopy shows esophageal varices. Underlying cause?
rationale: Esophageal varices usually result from portal hypertension, most often due to liver cirrhosis. Variceal bleeding is life-threatening and may require band ligation and vasoactive therapy.

text: A patient has severe vomiting followed by small-volume hematemesis after binge drinking. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Mallory-Weiss tear is a mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction after repeated retching or vomiting. It causes hematemesis, usually after alcohol binges.

text: A 30-year-old woman has ptosis and diplopia that worsen in the evening and improve with rest. Diagnosis?
rationale: Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular muscles first. Symptoms worsen with use and improve with rest. It is caused by antibodies against postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors.

text: A 67-year-old man has tremor when reaching for objects, wide-based gait, and dysmetria. Lesion is most likely in which area?
rationale: Intention tremor, dysmetria, and ataxia point to cerebellar dysfunction. Cerebellar lesions impair coordination rather than strength.

text: A 29-year-old woman has episodic headache, sweating, and palpitations with severe hypertension. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pheochromocytoma causes episodic headache, sweating, palpitations, and paroxysmal hypertension from catecholamine excess. Plasma free metanephrines are commonly used for screening.

text: A 40-year-old man has hypertension, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Primary hyperaldosteronism causes hypertension with hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis. Common causes include adrenal adenoma and bilateral adrenal hyperplasia.

text: A 33-year-old man has HIV with CD4 count 120. Which prophylaxis is indicated?
rationale: Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis with TMP-SMX is indicated when CD4 count is below 200. MAC prophylaxis is generally considered at lower CD4 counts depending on ART status.

text: A 22-year-old woman has dysuria and a positive NAAT for Chlamydia trachomatis. Recommended treatment?
rationale: Uncomplicated chlamydial infection is commonly treated with doxycycline. Sexual partners should also be treated, and patients should abstain until treatment is completed.

text: A 4-year-old has nephritic syndrome 2 weeks after impetigo. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis can follow streptococcal skin or throat infection by 1 to 3 weeks. It presents with hematuria, edema, and hypertension.

text: A 2-year-old has sudden episodes of crying, drawing up legs, vomiting, and currant jelly stool. First-line nonoperative treatment?
rationale: Intussusception is often treated first with air or contrast enema if there is no perforation or peritonitis. It is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

text: A child has fever, conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, cracked lips, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Main feared cardiac complication?
rationale: Kawasaki disease can lead to coronary artery aneurysms if not treated promptly with IVIG and aspirin.

text: A 30-year-old woman has frothy green vaginal discharge and a strawberry cervix. Diagnosis?
rationale: Trichomoniasis classically causes frothy green discharge and a strawberry cervix. It is treated with metronidazole, and sexual partners also require treatment.

text: A woman has thin gray vaginal discharge with a fishy odor and clue cells on microscopy. Diagnosis?
rationale: Bacterial vaginosis causes thin gray discharge with fishy odor and clue cells. It is treated with metronidazole. Vaginal pH is usually elevated.

text: A woman immediately after delivery has heavy bleeding and a boggy enlarged uterus. Most likely cause?
rationale: Uterine atony is the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage. Management includes uterine massage and uterotonic agents such as oxytocin.

text: A pregnant woman at 34 weeks has rupture of membranes and no contractions. Major fetal risk if delivery occurs before 37 weeks?
rationale: Preterm delivery increases risk of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome because of surfactant deficiency. Antenatal corticosteroids can improve fetal lung maturity.

text: A 60-year-old man has sudden severe epigastric pain and rigid abdomen. Upright chest X-ray shows free air under diaphragm. Diagnosis?
rationale: Free air under the diaphragm with acute abdomen strongly suggests perforated peptic ulcer. This is a surgical emergency.

text: A patient with severe pancreatitis develops left flank ecchymosis. This sign is called?
rationale: Grey Turner sign refers to flank ecchymosis and suggests retroperitoneal hemorrhage, as can occur in hemorrhagic pancreatitis.

text: A patient has a fall on an outstretched hand and an X-ray shows distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement. Diagnosis?
rationale: Colles fracture is a distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement, often after a fall on an outstretched hand. It is common in older adults.

text: A young man has wrist pain after a fall, and tenderness in the anatomic snuffbox. Diagnosis?
rationale: Snuffbox tenderness after a fall suggests scaphoid fracture. It may not appear immediately on X-ray, so immobilization is important because of avascular necrosis risk.

text: A 24-year-old woman has delusions, hallucinations, and prominent depressive episodes, with psychotic symptoms also occurring when mood symptoms are absent. Diagnosis?
rationale: Schizoaffective disorder includes both mood episodes and psychotic symptoms, with at least a period of psychosis occurring independently of mood symptoms.

text: A patient with major depression has suicidal ideation with a plan, refuses food, and is severely psychomotor retarded. Rapid effective treatment?
rationale: ECT is indicated when rapid response is needed in severe major depression, especially with suicidality, psychosis, or inability to eat and drink safely.

text: A 66-year-old man has chest pain that improves with nitroglycerin. Which diagnosis is most likely?
rationale: Stable angina classically causes exertional chest pain relieved by rest or nitroglycerin.

text: Which cardiac marker is most specific for myocardial infarction?
rationale: Cardiac troponin is the most specific biomarker for myocardial infarction.

text: A patient has atrial fibrillation. The most feared complication is?
rationale: Atrial fibrillation increases thromboembolic risk, especially ischemic stroke.

text: Which drug is first-line to terminate unstable ventricular tachycardia with pulse?
rationale: Unstable ventricular tachycardia with a pulse requires immediate synchronized cardioversion.

text: A 73-year-old has dyspnea, orthopnea, and elevated BNP. Diagnosis?
rationale: Dyspnea, orthopnea, and elevated BNP strongly suggest heart failure.

text: What is the most common cause of bronchiolitis in infants?
rationale: RSV is the most common cause of bronchiolitis in infants.

text: A COPD patient with chronic hypoxemia may benefit from?
rationale: Long-term oxygen therapy improves outcomes in selected patients with chronic severe hypoxemia.

text: A patient with asthma has wheeze and reversible obstruction. Best controller medication?
rationale: Inhaled corticosteroids are the main controller treatment for persistent asthma.

text: What is the best initial test for suspected pulmonary embolism in a hemodynamically stable patient?
rationale: CT pulmonary angiography is the usual diagnostic imaging test for stable suspected pulmonary embolism.

text: A 68-year-old smoker with hemoptysis and weight loss most likely has?
rationale: Hemoptysis and weight loss in an older smoker strongly suggest lung cancer.

text: A patient has epigastric burning pain relieved by food. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Duodenal ulcer pain often improves temporarily with meals.

text: H. pylori is associated with which condition?
rationale: Helicobacter pylori is a major cause of peptic ulcer disease.

text: A patient with severe RUQ pain, fever, and jaundice most likely has?
rationale: The triad of RUQ pain, fever, and jaundice suggests ascending cholangitis.

text: Which investigation is best first for suspected gallstones?
rationale: Ultrasound is the first-line imaging test for suspected gallstones.

text: A patient with cirrhosis and ascites should have initial diagnostic?
rationale: Diagnostic paracentesis is indicated in new or worsening ascites to assess for infection and cause.

text: A unilateral facial weakness sparing the forehead suggests?
rationale: Forehead sparing facial weakness indicates an upper motor neuron lesion such as stroke.

text: Which headache is described as the worst headache of life?
rationale: Thunderclap headache is classic for subarachnoid hemorrhage.

text: A patient with Parkinson disease most commonly has which tremor?
rationale: Parkinson disease classically causes a resting tremor.

text: Which medication is commonly used for acute status epilepticus first?
rationale: A benzodiazepine such as lorazepam is first-line initial treatment for status epilepticus.

text: The triad of gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, and dementia suggests?
rationale: Normal pressure hydrocephalus presents with gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline.

text: Which medication is first-line for type 2 diabetes in most patients?
rationale: Metformin is first-line pharmacotherapy for most patients with type 2 diabetes.

text: A patient with Graves disease most likely has?
rationale: Exophthalmos is strongly associated with Graves disease.

text: A patient with hypothyroidism commonly has?
rationale: Hypothyroidism commonly causes cold intolerance, fatigue, and weight gain.

text: Which emergency is associated with severe hyperglycemia, ketones, and acidosis?
rationale: Diabetic ketoacidosis causes hyperglycemia with ketones and metabolic acidosis.

text: A patient has moon face, buffalo hump, purple striae, and HTN. Diagnosis?
rationale: These features are classic for Cushing syndrome.

text: A patient has RBC casts in urine. This suggests?
rationale: RBC casts are strongly suggestive of glomerulonephritis.

text: Massive proteinuria, edema, and hypoalbuminemia indicate?
rationale: Nephrotic syndrome presents with heavy proteinuria, edema, and hypoalbuminemia.

text: Which electrolyte abnormality is most dangerous in renal failure due to arrhythmia risk?
rationale: Hyperkalemia is especially dangerous because it can cause life-threatening arrhythmias.

text: First test for suspected nephrolithiasis in many adults is?
rationale: Non-contrast CT KUB is commonly used to diagnose kidney stones.

text: A patient with pyelonephritis usually has?
rationale: Pyelonephritis typically presents with fever, flank pain, and urinary symptoms.

text: A patient with B12 deficiency may also have?
rationale: Vitamin B12 deficiency can cause neurologic symptoms in addition to megaloblastic anemia.

text: Which leukemia is associated with Philadelphia chromosome?
rationale: The Philadelphia chromosome is classically associated with chronic myeloid leukemia.

text: A patient has prolonged PTT and hemarthroses. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hemophilia A causes hemarthroses and prolonged PTT.

text: What is the hallmark smear finding in sickle cell disease?
rationale: Sickle cell disease is characterized by sickled red blood cells on smear.

text: A patient with ITP usually has?
rationale: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura typically causes isolated thrombocytopenia.

text: Morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour suggests?
rationale: Prolonged morning stiffness suggests inflammatory arthritis such as rheumatoid arthritis.

text: The classic joint in gout is?
rationale: Gout classically affects the first metatarsophalangeal joint.

text: A patient with temporal headache and jaw claudication most likely has?
rationale: Temporal headache with jaw claudication suggests giant cell arteritis.

text: Butterfly rash and photosensitivity suggest?
rationale: Malar rash and photosensitivity are classic features of SLE.

text: Ankylosing spondylitis mainly affects which area?
rationale: Ankylosing spondylitis primarily affects the axial skeleton and sacroiliac joints.

text: Which organism causes strep throat?
rationale: Group A Streptococcus is the usual cause of streptococcal pharyngitis.

text: The first-line treatment for uncomplicated cystitis is often?
rationale: Nitrofurantoin is a common first-line treatment for uncomplicated cystitis.

text: A painless genital ulcer suggests?
rationale: Primary syphilis classically presents with a painless chancre.

text: Which infection is associated with oral thrush and low CD4 count?
rationale: Opportunistic infections and oral thrush are common in advanced HIV infection.

text: RIPE therapy is used for?
rationale: RIPE therapy is used to treat tuberculosis.

text: Barking cough and inspiratory stridor in a child suggest?
rationale: Croup classically causes barking cough and inspiratory stridor.

text: Drooling, tripod position, and toxic appearance in a child suggest?
rationale: These are classic emergency signs of epiglottitis.

text: Projectile non-bilious vomiting in a 4-week-old suggests?
rationale: Projectile non-bilious vomiting in early infancy suggests pyloric stenosis.

text: Currant jelly stool in an infant suggests?
rationale: Currant jelly stool is classic for intussusception.

text: Fever for 5 days, conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, and rash suggest?
rationale: These findings strongly suggest Kawasaki disease.

text: The most feared complication of Kawasaki disease is?
rationale: Kawasaki disease can cause coronary artery aneurysms.

text: A continuous machine-like murmur suggests?
rationale: A patent ductus arteriosus classically causes a continuous machine-like murmur.

text: Poor eye contact, delayed language, and repetitive behavior suggest?
rationale: These are common features of autism spectrum disorder.

text: Koplik spots are seen in?
rationale: Koplik spots are characteristic of measles.

text: Respiratory distress syndrome in preterm infants is due to deficiency of?
rationale: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome is caused by surfactant deficiency.

text: Painless third-trimester bleeding most likely indicates?
rationale: Painless bleeding in late pregnancy suggests placenta previa.

text: Painful bleeding with a tender rigid uterus in pregnancy suggests?
rationale: Painful bleeding with uterine tenderness suggests placental abruption.

text: Seizures in a patient with preeclampsia indicate?
rationale: Preeclampsia with seizures is eclampsia.

text: First-line medication to prevent seizures in severe preeclampsia is?
rationale: Magnesium sulfate is used for seizure prophylaxis in severe preeclampsia.

text: Postmenopausal bleeding should be considered what until proven otherwise?
rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding should prompt evaluation for endometrial cancer.

text: Curdy white discharge with intense itching suggests?
rationale: Candida vaginitis causes pruritus and curdy white discharge.

text: Thin gray discharge with fishy odor and clue cells suggests?
rationale: Bacterial vaginosis causes thin gray malodorous discharge with clue cells.

text: Dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility suggest?
rationale: Endometriosis classically presents with dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility.

text: A patient with infertility, obesity, and hirsutism most likely has?
rationale: PCOS commonly causes infertility, obesity, and hirsutism.

text: A tender irreducible groin swelling with bowel obstruction suggests?
rationale: A painful irreducible hernia with obstructive symptoms suggests strangulation.

text: Free air under the diaphragm in acute abdomen most likely indicates?
rationale: Pneumoperitoneum on imaging suggests a perforated hollow viscus.

text: McBurney point tenderness is classic for?
rationale: McBurney point tenderness is classic for appendicitis.

text: The most common cause of small bowel obstruction in adults is?
rationale: Postoperative adhesions are the most common cause of small bowel obstruction in adults.

text: A fall on an outstretched hand causing distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement is?
rationale: A Colles fracture is a distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement.

text: Snuffbox tenderness after a fall suggests?
rationale: Anatomic snuffbox tenderness suggests scaphoid fracture.

text: A twisting knee injury with pop and swelling suggests?
rationale: A pop with rapid swelling after twisting knee injury suggests ACL tear.

text: Absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, and hypotension suggest?
rationale: This triad suggests tension pneumothorax requiring urgent decompression.

text: Hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and distended neck veins suggest?
rationale: Beck triad indicates cardiac tamponade.

text: Loss of interest and low mood for more than 2 weeks suggest?
rationale: Persistent low mood and anhedonia for over 2 weeks suggest major depressive disorder.

text: Decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, and risky behavior suggest?
rationale: These are classic symptoms of mania.

text: Recurrent panic attacks with fear of future attacks suggest?
rationale: Unexpected panic attacks with ongoing concern suggest panic disorder.

text: Excessive worry for over 6 months about many topics suggests?
rationale: Excessive difficult-to-control worry for at least 6 months suggests generalized anxiety disorder.

text: Hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized behavior for over 6 months suggest?
rationale: Persistent psychotic symptoms with functional decline for over 6 months suggest schizophrenia.

text: First treatment for opioid overdose is?
rationale: Naloxone is the antidote for opioid overdose.

text: Confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia in alcohol misuse suggest?
rationale: This triad suggests Wernicke encephalopathy due to thiamine deficiency.

text: Silvery plaques on extensor surfaces suggest?
rationale: Psoriasis causes well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale, especially on extensor surfaces.

text: Honey-colored crusted lesions around mouth suggest?
rationale: Impetigo causes honey-colored crusted lesions.

text: A pearly papule with telangiectasia on sun-exposed skin suggests?
rationale: Basal cell carcinoma often appears as a pearly papule with telangiectasia.

text: Burrows with intense nocturnal itching suggest?
rationale: Scabies classically causes burrows and worse itching at night.

text: ABCDE criteria are used for?
rationale: ABCDE criteria are used to assess suspicious pigmented lesions for melanoma.

text: Central vision loss in an elderly patient most likely suggests?
rationale: Age-related macular degeneration typically causes central vision loss.

text: Peripheral tunnel vision with raised intraocular pressure suggests?
rationale: Glaucoma commonly causes peripheral visual loss with elevated intraocular pressure.

text: A sudden painful red eye with halo vision suggests?
rationale: Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with painful red eye and halos around lights.

text: Bulging erythematous tympanic membrane in a febrile child suggests?
rationale: Acute otitis media often causes a bulging erythematous tympanic membrane.

text: Severe ear pain with tragus tenderness after swimming suggests?
rationale: Otitis externa is associated with tragal tenderness and water exposure.

text: Vertigo, tinnitus, and hearing loss suggest?
rationale: Ménière disease classically presents with episodic vertigo, tinnitus, and hearing loss.

text: Anaphylaxis first-line treatment is?
rationale: IM epinephrine is first-line treatment for anaphylaxis.

text: What is the antidote for acetaminophen overdose?
rationale: N-acetylcysteine is the antidote for acetaminophen toxicity.

text: Pinpoint pupils and respiratory depression suggest overdose with?
rationale: Opioid overdose classically causes miosis and respiratory depression.

text: Best screening test for average-risk colorectal cancer is?
rationale: Colonoscopy is a standard screening test for colorectal cancer in average-risk adults.

text: Annual screening for high-risk smokers is done with?
rationale: Low-dose CT is the recommended lung cancer screening tool for eligible high-risk smokers.

text: Which intervention most reduces COPD progression risk?
rationale: Smoking cessation is the most effective intervention to slow COPD progression.

text: A competent adult refuses blood transfusion for religious reasons. Best action?
rationale: A competent adult has the right to refuse treatment, even life-saving treatment.

text: If a patient threatens to kill an identifiable person, the physician may?
rationale: Imminent threat to an identifiable person can justify breaching confidentiality to protect safety.

text: Before accepting a risky refusal of treatment, the physician must assess?
rationale: Decision-making capacity should be assessed when a patient refuses important treatment.
